item.title: ARC Raiders: Last Entry Quest (Access Work Station, Hidden Key, Seed Deposit Box)
item.description:
The final quest of the Flashpoint update in ARC Raiders is Last Entry , which players can get from Shani . This quest focuses on finding more information that the researchers have on the Mantikor , and all of the objectives are in the Seed Vault . Similar to most quests in the game, players will have to complete this in one round. The objectives are not that difficult, but for players stuck at a certain point, the ARC Raiders guide below has everything you need.

Last Entry Quest Walkthrough in ARC Raiders

Being one of the shorter quests in the game and revolving entirely around the Seed Vault in Stella Montis , you can start a run with a free loadout and extract using the Airshaft right next to your objectives. You can also surrender if you do not spawn close to the Seed Vault, as you will not lose any items from your own inventory.

There is an alternative exit from the Seed Vault in case a Shredder is guarding the main entrance, and you cannot get out once you are inside.

Once you reach the Seed Vault, access the researcher’s log, which is located right outside the entrance of the vault. Interact with this panel to complete the first objective. Your second objective is to find the hidden key. For this, head west and turn left to find the researcher’s clothing hanging nearby. Search it to obtain the key.

With the key in your possession, head back to the entrance of the Seed Vault and open it if someone has not already done so. To open it, you will need a battery cell, which is randomly placed around the vault area but is never far and should be within a 50m radius. Once you find it, carry it and use it to open the door.

Head inside and continue straight, ignoring the vaults on the left and right that contain Assorted Seeds you can use to purchase items from Celeste . Open the Seed Vault box ahead, which drops the Official Shutdown Documentation that you need to bring back to Shani. Doing so completes the quest and rewards you with:

  • White Indigo (Warden Color)
  • Yellow Light Stick Blueprint
10 /10

ARC Raiders

Follow
Followed
Extraction
Shooter
Third-Person Shooter
Survival
Systems
OpenCritic Reviews
Top Critic Avg: 87 /100 Critics Rec: 93%
Released
October 30, 2025
ESRB
Teen / Violence, Blood, In-Game Purchases, Users Interact
Developer(s)
Embark Studios
Publisher(s)
Embark Studios
7 Images

WHERE TO PLAY

DIGITAL

Extraction, Shooter, Third-Person Shooter, Survival
Powered by

item.title: PC Gamers Have 6 Games They Can Claim Right Now Completely Free of Charge
item.description:
PC gamers that want to expand their digital libraries have a whopping six games to snag right now, as long as they are willing to use both Steam and the Epic Games Store. Steam, the Epic Games Store, and other digital storefronts ensure that PC gamers almost always have some freebie to claim, with dozens up for grabs every year.

On the Steam side of things, there are currently two games available for PC gamers to claim, both of which expire on April 6 at 12:00pm CST. There's Depths of Horror: Mushroom Day , a first-person horror game where players explore a creepy cave, and then there's House Flipper , a cozy game where players buy houses, renovate them, and attempt to sell them for a profit. Both games were well-received by Steam users, and anyone interested should be sure to snag them ASAP.

Free Steam Games Right Now

  • Depths of Horror: Mushroom Day (until April 6)
  • House Flipper (until April 6)

Depths of Horror: Mushroom Day and House Flipper are both free until April 6, but if you are someone that claims the latter for free and want more, it's worth pointing out that the game's many DLC packs are currently on sale. House Flipper 2 is also discounted on Steam for a limited time, though who knows if it will one day be given away for free on the platform like its predecessor.

Everything Free on the Epic Games Store Right Now

  • Together After Dark (until April 5)
  • Clone Drone in the Danger Zone (until April 9)
  • Tractor Racers (until April 12)
  • TOMAK: Save the Earth Regeneration (until April 16)

Meanwhile, Epic Games Store users currently have four games to claim at the time of this writing. Together After Dark is a co-op horror game that is free on the Epic Games Store until April 5, while Clone Drone in the Danger Zone is a voxel action game that's free until April 9. Tractor Racers is, as its name describes, a racing game with tractors, and TOMAK: Save the Earth Regeneration is a visual novel game that's free until April 16.

When Clone Drone in the Danger Zone is cycled out of the Epic Games Store free games lineup, it will be replaced by Prop Sumo . Prop Sumo is a party fighting game ala Super Smash Bros. that will be free from April 9 to April 16. A different game will replace Prop Sumo at that point, but we have no idea what that will be at the time of this writing.

Subscribe to the newsletter for curated free PC game deals

Want every major free-game giveaway covered? Subscribe to our newsletter for curated coverage of Steam, Epic Games Store, and other storefront freebies, plus deal roundups and DLC sale highlights — perfect for expanding your PC game library with no-cost or discounted picks.
By subscribing, you agree to receive newsletter and marketing emails, and accept our Terms of Use and Privacy Policy. You can unsubscribe anytime.

Epic Games Store free games are given away every Thursday at 10:00am CST, but lately there have been bonus free games to claim as well. The bonus free games on the Epic Games Store have added a lot of mystery to the EGS free games, and hopefully it continues. While the free games on the Epic Games Store and elsewhere aren't always going to appeal to everybody, you can't beat free, and it's great to get free games with no strings attached.

item.title: 8 Final Fantasy Games With The Best World-Building, Ranked
item.description:
Final Fantasy games are typically set in high fantasy, sci-fi, or a mixture of the two in terms of settings. Some games have better world-building skills than others, which may be a bit unfair because they have spinoffs, including Final Fantasy 4 . Even games with no spinoffs can have great lore, though, like Final Fantasy 16 .

The following Final Fantasy games will be ranked based on how deep their lore goes and how lived-in these worlds are. Are they generic settings, or do they go above and beyond? Final Fantasy 7 , Final Fantasy Tactics , and World of Final Fantasy are just a few examples, so let’s get to the bottom of this fantasy hole.

Rearrange the covers into the correct US release order.

Your Order

Results

—

—

—

More Games
Rearrange the covers into the correct US release order.
Easy (5)Medium (7)Hard (10)

Your Order

Results

—
— —
More Games

8 Final Fantasy 13

As Above, So Below

Final Fantasy 13
6 /10
Released
March 9, 2010

WHERE TO PLAY

DIGITAL

Developer(s)
Square Enix
Genre(s)
JRPG
Platform(s)
PS3, Xbox 360, PC
Powered by

Final Fantasy 13 was built on two worlds: one is a floating nest of civilization called Cocoon, which hangs over the less cultivated land of Gran Pulse. There are a lot of terms thrown into the game that are hard to parse since it is mostly a linear JRPG, but the two sequels did help expand the culture and lore to make things more sensible.

What started as a simple story of two worlds clashing later involved gods fighting over fate and the main character, Lightning, being thrust into a role of godhood herself. There’s time travel, alternate realities, cursed magic, and so much more. While Final Fantasy 13 is not the most beloved entry in the series, it’s hard not to applaud Square Enix for trying something different and expanding its ideas beyond the normal fantasy tropes.

7 Final Fantasy Crystal Chronicles

A Non-Stop Pilgrimage

Final Fantasy Crystal Chronicles
Action RPG
Systems
Released
February 9, 2004
ESRB
Teen // Fantasy Violence

Developer(s)
The Game Designers Studio
Genre(s)
Action RPG
Platform(s)
Nintendo GameCube
Powered by

Final Fantasy Crystal Chronicles was a spinoff action JRPG made for the GameCube, which let players create their hero and go at it alone or with three other friends. A full party was ideal because the lore explained that a miasma was poisoning the land, which is why it was important for four heroes to go on a journey to bring water to a powerful tree to quell the spreading darkness.

One player always had to carry the bucket of water to protect the party from the miasma, which was a good bit of lore to begin with. In this world, the miasma was never healed, forcing a seemingly never-ending pilgrimage to keep it at bay. Further sequels did not require the bucket, but overall, for such a small game, the team gave Final Fantasy Crystal Chronicles an interesting gimmick and backstory, complete with a mix of races, too.

6 World Of Final Fantasy

A Celebration Of The Franchise

World Of Final Fantasy
9 /10
Released
October 25, 2016

WHERE TO PLAY

DIGITAL

Developer(s)
Tose
Genre(s)
JRPG
Platform(s)
PS4, PS Vita, Xbox One, Switch, PC
Powered by

World of Final Fantasy was kind of like a celebration of the series right around the 30th anniversary. It featured two twins, Lann and Reynn, going into a book which contained a world inhabited by other Final Fantasy characters. Each game was represented by a hero and a small town or dungeon as if that were their entire game in one location.

Cloud from Final Fantasy 7 , Bartz from Final Fantasy 5 , and so on. Each kingdom has its own problems to solve with the help of the twins, who learned to control monsters by stacking them on their heads or riding them into turn-based battles. It’s a silly spinoff not meant to be taken seriously, but the idea of a magic land where all Final Fantasy games exist is a great idea for a game world.

5 Final Fantasy 14

To Eorzea And Beyond

Final Fantasy 14
Released
August 27, 2013

WHERE TO PLAY

DIGITAL

ESRB
T for Teen - Language, Mild Blood, Sexual Themes, Use of Alcohol, Violence
Genre(s)
MMORPG
Powered by

Final Fantasy 14 did not start as a celebration of everything Final Fantasy , but it has certainly become one over time. Originally released in 2010, followed by a reboot in 2013, Final Fantasy 14 started on the continent of Eorzea, but with several expansions, the game is so much bigger now. Between patches, expansions, and world events, this game has pulled in a lot of lore from other games.

For example, there was a time when players could get Magitek Armor from Final Fantasy 6 or visit a different version of Ivalice from Final Fantasy Tactics . Beyond the homages, the world of Eorzea is quite extensive, from huge sweeping plains to giant mountains to bustling cities filled with diverse races and airships taking to the sky. Thanks to its size as an MMO, Final Fantasy 14 definitely gives off the vibe of a world that real-world players could imagine living in.

4 Final Fantasy 10

Sports And Religion

Final Fantasy 10
Released
December 17, 2001

WHERE TO PLAY

DIGITAL
PHYSICAL

Developer(s)
Square Enix
Genre(s)
JRPG
Platform(s)
PS2
Powered by

Final Fantasy 10 is different from the rest of the series as it features a tropical set of islands set in a vast ocean. Also, instead of warring kingdoms, everyone was united over one religion that forced Summoners every so often to go on pilgrimages to appease a whale-like god named Sin to stop his rampage. Instead of kingdoms, areas settled their disputes over an aquatic soccer-like game called Blitzball, and team loyalty was high.

Also, this was a world afraid of machines, as that’s how Sin originally appeared when humanity worshiped machines over gods. The sequel doesn’t expand the lore too much beyond going over what life is like after the finale, but overall, the tropical paradise of Spira in Final Fantasy 10 is a great one among all JRPGs.

3 Final Fantasy 9

The Definition Of Final Fantasy In The Gaming Dictionary

Final Fantasy 9
Released
July 7, 2000

WHERE TO PLAY

DIGITAL

Developer(s)
Square Enix
Genre(s)
JRPG
Platform(s)
PS4, Xbox One, Switch, PS1, Android, iOS, PC
Powered by

Final Fantasy 9 takes place on Gaia, which is an equivalent of Earth, filled with giant kingdoms and a diverse set of races. Exploring the world map feels more alive than most games in the series because airships can often be seen overhead, as that’s the major mode of transportation.

Even though the races aren’t detailed, and the kingdoms aren’t fully established, Final Fantasy 9 does a lot visually to paint a picture. It’s a full fantasy adventure that best represents the Final Fantasy series as a whole, with the only thing missing being visiting elemental crystals. It’s the small details that make the world light up, like the Moogle delivery system or explaining what a Black Mage is.

2 Final Fantasy Tactics

The Power Of Ivalice

Final Fantasy Tactics
Tactical
RPG
Systems
Released
June 20, 1997
ESRB
T For Teen // Mild Language, Violence

Developer(s)
Square
Genre(s)
Tactical, RPG
Platform(s)
PlayStation (Original)
Powered by

Final Fantasy Tactics takes place in the land of Ivalice, far into a state where the world is in a rough shape. There are extinct races, like Moogles, once thriving floating continents now laid destroyed, abandoned mechanized robots, and so much more, all of which are uncovered through side quests. The cool thing about this game is that the universe was expanded upon to a main entry, Final Fantasy 12 , which depicted the lost civilization from these side quests.

There were also two Final Fantasy Tactics sequels, although their version of Ivalice is more imaginary and confusing about how they fit into the timeline. Also, Vagrant Story , a hybrid turn-based and action JRPG, belongs in Ivalice as well. The core world of Final Fantasy Tactics is also impressive, with all of the political intrigue of warring families, but how Square Enix has expanded things is even more amazing.

1 Final Fantasy 7

An Expanding Universe

Final Fantasy 7
Released
January 31, 1997

WHERE TO PLAY

DIGITAL
PHYSICAL

ESRB
T for Teen: Blood, Fantasy Violence, Language, Mild Suggestive Themes
Genre(s)
RPG
Powered by

Final Fantasy 7 , without a doubt, has the biggest solo universe within the franchise. There are spinoffs galore from games to movies to books and beyond. At its core, Final Fantasy 7 was about a group of vigilantes who wanted to save the planet from being sucked dry by an evil corporation, Shinra, or destroyed by a madman, Sephiroth, who thought it was his duty to reset civilization.

The sequels and prequels all help fill in the gaps between organizations and individual characters like Zack, an old ally of the protagonist Cloud, who got his own game via Crisis Core: Final Fantasy 7 . With a third remake on the way, Final Fantasy 7 is a universe far from complete, and it may never be.

item.title: Daredevil: Born Again Season 2 is Once Again Asking the Eternal Question
item.description:
The second season of Daredevil: Born Again has been a massive improvement over its predecessor, arguably reaching the same level of quality as the Netflix series that was truly beloved among Marvel fans. Fight scenes are far more fun to watch play out, more time is spent with a suited-up Matt Murdock, and returning characters from the original show have larger roles — all improvements fans hoped the second season would make. With Daredevil having epic battles aboard boats and inside prison camps, not to mention a third season of Daredevil: Born Again being in the works, it’s only a matter of time before fans beg for a game about the Man Without Fear yet again.

Obviously, there is no indication that a Daredevil video game will be happening any time soon, with no rumors suggesting such a title is in development. It’s simply a mixture of wishful thinking, the character’s enduring popularity, and how well of a fit the Defender of Hell’s Kitchen would be for a video game that leads to the endless demands for such a project. For now, all fans can do is keep asking when he’ll finally get his due and become more than just a member of ensemble games like Marvel Rivals and Ultimate Alliance , though with Born Again thriving, it would certainly make sense for such a game to be greenlit sooner rather than later.

In 2004, a Daredevil game was in the works (likely attempting to capitalize on the buzz from the Ben Affleck movie) but was ultimately scrapped, serving as the only attempt at a solo game based on the character.

What Would a Daredevil Game Be Like?

It’s not too difficult to imagine how a Daredevil video game could play, as the character’s double life enables a good variety of mechanics and some superhero game staples. A Daredevil video game could feature:

  • Combo-focused combat and stealth mechanics comparable to Batman: Arkham and Marvel’s Spider-Man .
  • An open-world version of Hell’s Kitchen that takes advantage of Daredevil’s billy club traversal.
  • A deep rogue’s gallery for side stories and boss fights, including Kingpin and Bullseye alongside slightly deeper cuts like Typhoid Mary, Mr. Fear, and The Owl.
  • Choice-based lawyer segments where players can succeed or fail at defending Matt Murdock’s clients, allowing for (or costing players) some extra upgrades.
  • Puzzles that make use of Daredevil’s heightened senses, with tracking via his enhanced smell and eavesdropping via his sharp hearing also being viable. Radar vision could be another mechanic.
Drag weapons to fill the grid

Loadout Logic

Tap on a weapon to rotate it or press R while dragging.

Time —
Filled 0/36

Results

0
—
More Games
Drag weapons to fill the grid
EasyMediumHard

Loadout Logic

Tap on a weapon to rotate it or press R while dragging.
Time —
Filled 0/36
Results
0
—
More Games

From a narrative standpoint, any developer that takes on a hypothetical Daredevil game would be spoiled for inspiration. While the character has had a few mediocre runs here and there, like Diggle and Ahmed, a vast majority of Daredevil runs have been assigned classic status from comic readers. Second to maybe only Batman in terms of consistent quality, a game could find success by putting a spin on elements introduced by virtualy any of the character’s best writers. A straightforward remix of Miller’s work could work, as could a bolder idea like Bendis’ Kingpin Murdock or the prison arc from Brubaker’s run. Waid’s swashbuckling and lighthearted story could serve as a fun change of pace for a game if the goal is to stand out from the MCU shows, while Soule’s run could enable an NPC sidekick via Blindspot, or Zdarsky’s run could allow for a second playable Daredevil via Elektra. Really, the sky is the limit for a Daredevil game, so much so that it’s easy to imagine a multi-game franchise centered around the vigilante lawyer.

The Perfect Developer For a Daredevil Game is Just as Obvious Now as It Was Years Ago

If it’s so easy for fans to think up a Daredevil game, surely it’s only a matter of time before it’s seen as a safe investment from Marvel Games and whatever studio it chooses for the task. And though it has its hands full with the upcoming Marvel’s Wolverine — a game that might just have a Daredevil cameo — not to mention a leaked X-Men game and Marvel’s Spider-Man 3 , Insomniac Games really is a perfect fit. It’s building its own connected Marvel universe, Spider-Man’s combat would be a great starting point for Daredevil, and Marvel’s Wolverine seems poised to prove the studio can nail a slightly darker tone. Just like Insomniac was the name thrown around for a Daredevil game when the Netflix show was at its peak levels of interest back in 2018, this studio remains the best choice for such a game today. And though Insomniac’s slate is quite full at the moment, seeing Daredevil finally get the game he deserves - even if it’s years away - would undoubtedly be worth the wait.

5 Questions
Daredevil Is Ready for His Solo Gaming Spotlight... AreYou?
Your Top Score
--
Attempts
--

0
0
Report Error

Found an error? Send it info@gamerant.com so it can be corrected.

item.title: This is The Ultimate Team For Pokemon Ruby and Sapphire, From Badges to Endgame
item.description:
It has been almost 25 years since the release of Pokemon Ruby and Sapphire , and after all this time, Gen 3 remains one of the most beloved and iconic for millions of fans. Whether you grew up with the difficult choice of picking Treecko, Torchic, or Mudkip as a starter, or maybe you spent weeks looking for Mirage Island hoping it would show up for you, Gen 3 games had a lot to offer and were very unique when they came out. Pokemon Ruby and Sapphire on Switch and Switch 2 may be more than a pipe dream now that Pokemon FireRed and LeafGreen received ports, so there's a very good chance these games will return.

Between the National Dex in FireRed and LeafGreen being impossible to complete without Gen 3 ports and the fact that Pokemon Ruby and Sapphire were leaked for the Switch, it all seems a matter of when rather than if. It's unclear whether Emerald will also return, but it's a bit of a different experience from the base Gen 3 titles in terms of encounters, main battles, and more. As such, if you're looking for the best team to use in Pokemon Ruby and Sapphire , keep in mind that it won't be the same as Emerald.

But yes, there is an ultimate team you can use in Pokemon Ruby and Sapphire on Switch, and it's all about maximizing your team lineup from early in the game all the way to the showdown with the Elite Four and the Champion. While this journey can be completed with all starters, this team is about optimization, and the starter of choice is Mudkip.

Swampert: The Best Starter in Pokemon Ruby and Sapphire

Swampert is the best starter in Pokemon Ruby and Sapphire purely from an encounter point of view, as it's never a poor choice against the Gym Leaders and Elite Four in Hoenn. There are some trainers with Grass-type Pokemon, and while Swampert takes four times the damage from Grass, it's the only weakness it has. With moves like Surf, Earthquake, Ice Beam, and Brick Break, Swampert can clear the entire game on its own, and you may not even need other Pokemon to do it. Ice Beam covers Swampert's only weakness, since Ice is supereffective against Grass, and it's also great against trainers like Winona and Drake.

How to Get Swampert Early in Pokemon Ruby and Sapphire

  • Evolve Mudkip into Marshtomp at level 16, and then into Swampert at level 36.

Gardevoir: A Special Sweeper That Carries You From Route 102 to Endgame

Pokemon 's Hoenn region offers the opportunity to catch many of its native Pokemon in the first few areas, and such is the case for Ralts, which can be found very early in the game, albeit it's rarer than the other critters at a 4% spawn rate. Ralts can then evolve into Gardevoir over time, and you can teach it incredibly strong moves like Psychic, Thunderbolt, and Calm Mind. With the latter, it gets a boost to both its Special Attack and Special Defense, quickly becoming a sweeper in high-stakes, longer battles. You could also teach it Thief for coverage and for collecting items for free during your playthrough.

How to Get Gardevoir Early in Pokemon Ruby and Sapphire

  • Catch Ralts on Route 102, evolve it into Kirlia at level 20, and into Gardevoir at level 30

Swellow: A Powerful Carry With One-Shot Options

Even before you get to the first Gym in the game, you can get one of the best Pokemon in Ruby and Sapphire in the form of Taillow. This little bird Pokemon can evolve into Swellow, become your main Fly user for both convenience and battle purposes, and carry you through the hardest battles in the game with the incredible combo of Guts plus Facade. Guts is an ability that allows Swellow to deal more damage if it's affected by a status condition, and Facade is a move that does the same, essentially allowing you to inflict Swellow with status conditions purposefully and then spam Facade to win every fight. This is easier said than done at times, but it can be your secret weapon through the Elite Four (minus Phoebe).

You could replace Swellow with Salamence in Ruby and Sapphire's late-game, but it's not necessary. Swellow can still do plenty of damage on its own.

How to Get Swellow Early in Pokemon Ruby and Sapphire

  • Catch Taillow on Route 104 or in Petalburg Woods, then evolve it into Swellow at level 22.

Breloom: One of The Best Grass-Type Pokemon Ever, and It Packs a Punch

Breloom is arguably one of the best Grass-type Pokemon ever, as it's pretty speedy, has good offensive stats, can deal serious damage with its Fighting-type moves, and it has access to Spore. This is only partly true in Pokemon Ruby and Sapphire , though, as Breloom never learns Spore, but you can teach it to its pre-evolution at level 54 if you never evolve it before. This is a big problem and something that is almost never worth doing in a regular playthrough, but Breloom is very strong regardless. With moves like Leech Seed for sustain, Bulk Up for sweeping potential, and Mach Punch for priority, it's always a fantastic choice.

How to Get Breloom Early in Pokemon Ruby and Sapphire

  • Catch Shroomish in Petalburg Woods, then evolve it into Breloom at level 23.

Torkoal: An Endgame Powerhouse With Amazing Stats

Even though you get Torkoal relatively early, it shines the more you train it. Even if you don't spend time EV-training it, it comes with very good stats from the get-go, with 70 HP, 85 Attack, 140 Defense, 85 Special Attack, 70 Special Defense, and 20 Speed. It is very slow, especially compared to other Pokemon on the team, like Breloom and Swellow, but it has a lot of bulk and options to become a hybrid attacker even if its primary use would be as a Fire-type Pokemon. It can learn great moves like Flamethrower, Heat Wave, and Overheat for Fire coverage, and then Body Slam and Sludge Bomb on the physical side. With 85 in both attacking stats, a speed-impairing nature would be the best for this critter in order to give it higher stats in other areas.

How to Get Torkoal Early in Pokemon Ruby and Sapphire

  • Catch Torkoal on Fiery Path, after Route 111.

Absol: A Physical Sweeper That Solos The Elite Four

The last Pokemon on the team is Absol, and you can get it towards the middle part of the game. It may not seem like a great pick when considering Pokemon FireRed and LeafGreen 's type chart and type split applies to Ruby and Sapphire as well, because Dark is a special type and Absol's Special Attack is very low. However, it has naturally very high Attack, and it learns Swords Dance. This makes it a fantastic physical sweeper that can then destroy the game with moves like Shadow Ball, Return, and Aerial Ace. Even its Fighting weakness is not a big deal if you teach it Aerial Ace, as it will most likely one-shot opposing Fighting-type Pokemon. Shadow Ball also one-shots Phoebe's entire team after Swords Dance, and Return can one-shot many mons that don't resist Normal. Just be careful when you try to catch one, as it can wipe out your whole team.

How to Get Absol Early in Pokemon Ruby and Sapphire

  • Catch Absol on Route 120.
Pokemon Ruby and Sapphire
RPG
Systems
Released
March 19, 2003
ESRB
e
Developer(s)
Game Freak
Publisher(s)
Nintendo
Engine
Unreal Engine

WHERE TO PLAY

PHYSICAL

Genre(s)
RPG
Powered by
